faq.title: What you need to know
faq.a1: No. PreEnvios is 100% free. We just help you compare so you can choose the best option. Fees are charged by each provider and we show them before you send.
faq.q2: How do you update the rates?
faq.a2: We check rates directly from each provider multiple times a day. What you see here is what you get when you send.
faq.a3: We compare Remitly, Wise, Xoom, Ria, WorldRemit, Western Union and MoneyGram for corridors USA → Honduras, USA → Dominican Republic, USA → Guatemala, USA → El Salvador, USA → Colombia and USA → Mexico.
faq.q4: How long does a transfer take?
faq.a4: It depends on the provider and method. Some are instant, others take up to 1 business day. We show the estimated time in each result.
faq.a5: Yes. We started with Honduras, Dominican Republic, Guatemala, El Salvador, Colombia and Mexico, and plan to expand to more Latin American corridors soon.
faq.q6: Why do some operators appear first?
faq.a6: The order of results considers the exchange rate, transfer speed and commercial agreements with some operators. Preenvios.com may receive a commission when you click and complete a transfer. This comes at no additional cost to you.

footer.desc: Compare remittance providers before sending money to Latin America. Save on every transfer.
footer.disclaimerText: PreEnvios.com is an informational comparison tool. We are not a financial institution, we do not process payments, receive or send money. Published rates are approximate and may differ from the final amount offered by each provider at the time of transaction. Results may be ordered according to affiliate agreements. Preenvios.com does not guarantee that the rates shown are final at the time of transfer.

disclaimers.d1: Rates shown are approximate and may vary at the time of transaction. Always confirm the final amount directly with the remittance provider before sending.
disclaimers.d2: PreEnvios.com is not a financial institution. We do not process payments, receive or send money. We only compare public information from remittance providers.
disclaimers.d3: The order of results may be influenced by commercial agreements with operators.
disclaimers.d4: PreEnvios may receive a commission when you click and complete a transfer. This comes at no additional cost to you and does not affect the ranking order.
disclaimers.d5: PreEnvios is not responsible for losses resulting from decisions made based on the information displayed. Users must exercise their own judgment and verify data before transferring money. By using this site, you accept these terms.
disclaimers.d6: The logos and names of remittance providers shown are nominative trademarks used for informational comparison purposes. PreEnvios is not affiliated with, sponsored by, or endorsed by these companies unless otherwise indicated.

nosotros.heroTitle: We're an independent remittance comparison tool built by and for the Latino diaspora
nosotros.heroLede: We don't handle money. We just help you pick the best provider before you send.
nosotros.storyP1: PreEnvios started from personal frustration: the Latino diaspora in the United States sends more than $150 billion a year to their families, and almost no one compares providers before transferring. Many lose $5, $10, $20 per transfer just by sticking with the first option they find.
nosotros.storyP2: We built PreEnvios in 2025 to change that. One place to compare rates, fees and delivery speed for the most-used remittance providers — free, no signup, no hidden fees.
nosotros.missionText: No Latino family should lose money on a transfer for lack of information. We want to be the go-to comparison tool for the entire diaspora in the U.S. — Honduras, Dominican Republic, Guatemala, El Salvador, Colombia, Mexico and more corridors coming.
nosotros.v1Desc: We publish how we make money, how we rank results and which agreements we have with each provider. No fine print.
nosotros.v2Desc: We are not owned by any remittance provider or bank. We show every option, even the ones that don't pay us a commission.
nosotros.v3Desc: Seeing the best provider should take 10 seconds. No forms, no signups, no distracting ads. You pick fast and send.
nosotros.v4Desc: We never charge users. Not now, not ever. Our model is sustained by affiliate commissions paid by providers when you complete a transfer.
nosotros.revenueText: We receive an affiliate commission when you click "Send now" and complete a transfer with a partner provider. This costs you nothing extra and does not affect the ranking order.
nosotros.revenueLink: See the details in How we earn money →
nosotros.ctaText: Write to us. We reply to everything — users, press, providers, investors.

cookies.title: We use cookies to improve your experience
cookies.description: PreEnvios uses first-party cookies and Google Analytics to measure how people use the comparator and improve it. We don't sell your data. You choose what to accept.
cookies.settingsDescription: Here you can choose which cookies you accept. The necessary ones are always on because the site doesn't work without them.
cookies.categoryNecessaryDesc: Basic cookies for the site to work: remembering your language, your preferred country, and your session if you start one. These can't be disabled.
cookies.categoryAnalyticsDesc: Google Analytics tells us what works and what doesn't. Example: how many people found the cheapest remittance. Anonymous and aggregated.
cookies.categoryMarketingDesc: We don't use marketing cookies today. In the future we might measure ad conversions (Google Ads, Meta). You decide whether to allow them from now.

seccion0.alertasSubtitle: Notifications when HNL/USD goes up — send when it goes farther

stats.tag: Sending money from the US to Honduras
stats.title: Honduras and remittances from the United States in 2026
stats.lede: 26% of Honduras's GDP comes from remittances sent by the diaspora in the United States. Every dollar that arrives sustains a family, a small business, an education. Honduras is one of the Central American countries most dependent on remittances — these are the numbers that prove it. Data from the Central Bank of Honduras (BCH) 2025 and CEPAL 2024.

editorial.tag: The Honduran diaspora in the United States
editorial.titleLine2: From Houston to San Pedro Sula.
editorial.titleLine3: Sending money to Honduras is more than a transaction.
editorial.p1: Hondurans in the United States send roughly $8.5 billion home each year. That money pays for tuition in Comayagua, builds homes in La Ceiba, and keeps small businesses running in Choluteca.
editorial.p2: The difference between one provider and another can be as much as $10 on every $200 sent. Multiply that by the 8 to 12 transfers many families make each year — and the savings come out to between $80 and $120 staying with your family every year.

ciudades.title: Sending money to cities in Honduras: 6 main areas
ciudades.lede: 6 main cities in Honduras where your family likely receives. All have coverage from the major providers (Remitly, Xoom, Western Union, MoneyGram) at banks and agencies. Rural areas may require Tigo Money or a bank deposit at the nearest urban branch.

errores.title: Mistakes when sending money to Honduras that cost up to $50
errores.lede: Each of these mistakes has cost a Honduran between $5 and $50. Learn them before your next transfer.

items.texto: The best provider changes week to week depending on the rate. Comparing every time saves you $5–$10 per transfer.

"$0 fee" doesn't mean a free transfer. The provider makes up for it with a worse exchange rate — you end up receiving fewer lempiras. Always look at the final lempiras received, not just the dollar fee.

items.titulo: Paying Express when there's no rush
items.texto: "Express" in 5 minutes costs $5–$8 extra vs "Economy" in 1 business day. If your family doesn't need the money TODAY, Express is wasted money. Save it for real emergencies.
If the name doesn't match character by character with the Honduran ID, the transfer is held and can take days. Copy it exactly — accents, second surnames, everything.
Providers charge an extra 2–4% because card processors (Visa/Mastercard) charge them high fees. On a $200 transfer, that's up to $8 lost. Use debit or ACH (direct bank transfer).

items.titulo: Sending on Friday or the weekend
items.texto: The BCH doesn't operate on Saturdays or Sundays. The rate "freezes" at Friday's value, which historically tends to be the worst of the week due to bank closures. Send Monday or Tuesday for the best rate.

faq.title: Frequently asked questions from Hondurans in the US

items.q: How many lempiras do I get for $200 USD?
items.a: At the current Central Bank of Honduras (BCH) rate, $200 equals the theoretical lempiras shown above multiplied by 200. But each provider applies a margin: the best operator delivers roughly 1–2% less, and the worst up to 3–4% less. Real difference: up to 70 lempiras ($2.80 USD) on a single $200 transfer.

items.q: Which Honduran banks receive remittances?
items.a: The 5 main ones are BAC Credomatic, Banco Atlántida, Ficohsa, Banpaís, and Banco del País. All major providers (Remitly, Xoom, WorldRemit) deposit directly to these accounts. If your family doesn't have an account, Tigo Money or cash pickup are the alternatives.

items.q: Does Tigo Money work everywhere in Honduras?
items.a: Yes. Tigo has nationwide coverage, and Tigo Money operates in all 18 departments of the country. Especially useful in rural areas where there's no bank nearby: Lempira, Intibucá, Ocotepeque, La Paz. Regulated by the CNBS.

items.q: Is there a tax on remittances in Honduras?
items.a: No. Honduras doesn't tax remittances received. Remittances are exempt from personal income tax (ISR). The only cost is the provider's fee and the margin on the exchange rate.

items.q: How long does a transfer take to arrive?
items.aIntro: Depends on the method and provider:
aBullets: Cash (Western Union, Ria): minutes after receiving the MTCN code
Bank deposit: 2–24 hours depending on the receiving bank

items.q: How much arrives in Honduras if I send $100 USD?
items.a: At the current BCH rate, $100 equals the theoretical lempiras shown above multiplied by 100. Providers deliver between 1% and 4% less depending on the operator and method. Difference: up to 30 lempiras ($1.15 USD) per transfer — use the comparison tool to see who delivers more today.

items.q: Which is the best provider to send to Honduras?
items.a: There's no single "best" — it depends on the amount, urgency, and method. Remitly tends to win for amounts under $500 with cash pickup. Wise wins on bank deposits with a rate close to the BCH. Xoom and WorldRemit are competitive with promotions for new users. Use the comparison tool above this page to see who delivers more lempiras today.

cta.titleLine1: Ready to send money to Honduras.
cta.lede: With what you've learned in this guide, your next transfer to Honduras can save you $8–$15 vs using the first provider that pops up on Google. Multiplied by 12 transfers a year, that's $100+ staying with your family.
checklist: Send Monday/Tuesday · avoid Friday/weekends
Debit or ACH > credit card
Look at lempiras received, not just the fee
Confirm the name exactly as on the ID
Save the MTCN or tracking ID of every transfer
Compare every transfer — the best provider changes week to week

fuentes.label: Sources:
fuentes.text: Macroeconomic data updated annually. HNL/USD rate updated daily. Last content update: {fechaActualizacion}.

seccion0.bancoNombre: Central Bank of the Dominican Republic
seccion0.alertasSubtitle: Notifications when DOP/USD goes up — send when it goes farther

stats.tag: Sending money from the US to the Dominican Republic
stats.title: The Dominican Republic and remittances from the United States in 2026
stats.lede: Every year, $10.3 billion crosses from Dominican neighborhoods in the Bronx, Washington Heights, Lawrence, and Miami to homes in Santo Domingo, Santiago, and the small towns of the Cibao. It's the largest remittance corridor in the Caribbean — and one of the most mature in the world. Data from the Central Bank of the Dominican Republic (BCRD) 2024 and IDB 2024.

editorial.tag: The Dominican diaspora in the United States
editorial.titleLine1: From Upper Manhattan to Santo Domingo.
editorial.titleLine3: A corridor with 50 years of history — and differences that matter.
editorial.p1: Dominican migration to the United States started in earnest in the 1960s. Five decades later, there are 2.4 million Dominicans in the country, concentrated in New York, New Jersey, Massachusetts, and Florida. It's one of the most established remittance corridors in the world — Quisqueyana, Caribe Express, and Pronto Envíos have been operating it for decades.
editorial.p2: But the international comparison set (Remitly, Wise, Xoom, WorldRemit) tends to deliver more pesos per dollar than traditional operators — the difference can be up to $8 on every $200 sent. Across 12 transfers a year, that's $96 staying with your family in the DR instead of paying margin to the operator.

ciudades.title: Sending money to cities in DR: 6 main destinations
ciudades.lede: 6 destinations where your family likely receives. All major operators (Remitly, Xoom, WorldRemit, Western Union, MoneyGram) have nationwide coverage via deposits to Banco Popular, BHD, Banreservas, and Scotiabank. For more remote areas in the south or deep Cibao, cash pickup at WU/MG remains the most reliable option.

errores.title: Mistakes when sending money to DR that cost up to $50
errores.lede: Each of these mistakes has cost a Dominican between $5 and $50. Learn them before your next transfer.

items.titulo: Sticking with the same provider you've always used
items.texto: Quisqueyana or Caribe Express are tradition — but digital providers tend to deliver 1–2% more pesos per dollar. On a $200 transfer, that's up to 250 extra pesos (RD$250 ≈ $4.10 USD) reaching your family.
"$0 fee" doesn't mean a free transfer. The provider makes up for it with a worse exchange rate — your family ends up receiving fewer pesos. Always compare final pesos received, not the dollar fee.

items.titulo: Paying Express when there's no rush
items.texto: "Express" in 5 minutes costs $5–$8 extra vs "Economy" in 1 business day. If your family doesn't need the money TODAY, Express is wasted money. Save it for real emergencies.

In the DR, two surnames are used (paternal and maternal). If the name doesn't match character by character with the ID, the transfer is held and can take days. Copy it exactly — accents, second surname, everything.
Providers charge an extra 2–4% when you pay with credit because card processors charge them high fees. On a $200 transfer, that's up to $8 lost. Use debit or ACH (direct bank transfer).
The BCRD doesn't operate on Saturdays or Sundays. The rate "freezes" at Friday's value — historically the worst of the week due to closures. Send Monday or Tuesday for a better rate.

faq.title: Frequently asked questions from Dominicans in the US

items.q: How many pesos do I get for $200 USD?
items.a: At the current Central Bank of the Dominican Republic (BCRD) rate, $200 equals the theoretical pesos shown above multiplied by 200. But each provider applies a margin: the best operator delivers roughly 1–2% less, and the worst up to 3–4% less. Real difference: up to 500 pesos ($8.30 USD) on a single $200 transfer.

items.q: Which Dominican banks receive remittances?
items.a: The main ones are Banco Popular Dominicano, BHD, Banreservas, Scotiabank, Banco Santa Cruz, and APAP. All major providers (Remitly, Xoom, WorldRemit, Wise) deposit directly to these accounts. If your family doesn't have an account, cash pickup at WU/MG agencies is the alternative.

items.q: Are Caribe Express and Quisqueyana included in the comparison?
items.a: No. The comparison covers the 7 global operators (Remitly, Wise, Xoom, WorldRemit, Western Union, MoneyGram, Ria) that dominate most of the Dominican market today. Traditional operators (Quisqueyana, Caribe Express, Pronto Envíos) have a strong presence in NYC but tend to fall behind on rate vs the digital ones — it's worth comparing.

items.q: Is there a tax on remittances in DR?
items.a: No. The Dominican Republic doesn't tax remittances received. They're exempt from personal income tax (ISR). The only cost is the provider's fee plus the margin on the exchange rate.

items.q: How long does a transfer take to arrive?
items.aIntro: Depends on the method and provider:
aBullets: Cash (Western Union, Ria, MoneyGram): minutes after receiving the code
Bank deposit: 2–24 hours depending on the receiving bank

items.q: How much arrives in DR if I send $100 USD?
items.a: At the current BCRD rate, $100 equals the theoretical pesos shown above multiplied by 100. Providers deliver between 1% and 4% less depending on the operator and method. Difference: up to 250 pesos ($4.10 USD) per transfer — use the comparison tool to see who delivers more today.

items.q: Which is the best provider to send to DR?
items.a: There's no single "best" — it depends on the amount, urgency, and method. Remitly tends to win for amounts under $500 with cash pickup. Wise is typically better for bank deposits thanks to a rate close to the BCRD. Xoom and WorldRemit are competitive with promotions for new users. Western Union is still the most reliable option for cash pickup in remote areas. Use the comparison tool above to see who delivers more pesos today.

cta.titleLine1: Ready to send money to DR.
cta.lede: With what you've learned in this guide, your next transfer to DR can save you $8–$15 vs using the first provider that pops up on Google. Multiplied by 12 transfers a year, that's $100+ staying with your family.
checklist: Send Monday/Tuesday · avoid Friday/weekends
Debit or ACH > credit card
Look at pesos received, not just the fee
Confirm the name exactly as on the ID (with both surnames)
Save the MTCN or tracking ID of every transfer
Compare every transfer — the best provider changes week to week

fuentes.text: Macroeconomic data updated annually. DOP/USD rate updated daily. Last content update: {fechaActualizacion}.

seccion0.alertasSubtitle: Notifications when GTQ/USD goes up — send when it goes farther

stats.tag: Sending money from the US to Guatemala
stats.title: Guatemala and remittances from the United States in 2026
stats.lede: Guatemala receives $21.5 billion a year from the United States — the largest remittance corridor in Central America, almost double Honduras. Those quetzales support families in Quetzaltenango, Huehuetenango, Cobán, and the country's 22 departments. Data from the Bank of Guatemala (Banguat) 2024 and CEPAL 2024.

editorial.tag: The Guatemalan diaspora in the United States
editorial.titleLine1: From Los Angeles to the capital.
editorial.titleLine3: All of Central America watches the largest corridor in the region.
editorial.p1: There are 1.8 million Guatemalans in the United States. Most live in California, Texas, Florida, and New York — and many come from the highland departments: Huehuetenango, San Marcos, Quiché. Each year they send $21.5 billion home, almost 19% of the country's GDP. It's the largest remittance corridor in Central America.
editorial.p2: But that scale hides a complexity: bank coverage varies a lot by department. Banrural dominates the rural highlands, while Banco Industrial and BAM concentrate the urban areas. Choosing the right provider based on where your family receives can save you up to $10 on every $200 sent — multiplied across the year's transfers, that's hundreds of quetzales staying with your family.

ciudades.title: Sending money to cities in Guatemala: 6 main areas
ciudades.lede: 6 destinations where your family likely receives. The major providers (Remitly, Xoom, WorldRemit, WU, MG) cover the entire country via Banrural, Banco Industrial, and BAM. In rural highland areas (Huehuetenango, Quiché, San Marcos), Banrural still has the broadest coverage — confirm your provider deposits there before paying for Express.

errores.title: Mistakes when sending money to Guatemala that cost up to $60
errores.lede: Each of these mistakes has cost a Guatemalan between $5 and $60. Learn them before your next transfer.

items.titulo: Not confirming which bank your family uses
items.texto: If the provider doesn't deposit to Banrural but your family only has an account there, you end up paying an extra fee for cash pickup or switching operators. Confirm BEFORE which bank the recipient uses.
"$0 fee" doesn't mean a free transfer. The provider makes up for it with a worse exchange rate — you end up receiving fewer quetzales. Look at the final quetzales received, not just the dollar fee.

items.titulo: Paying Express when there's no rush
items.texto: "Express" in 5 minutes costs $5–$8 extra vs "Economy" in 1–2 days. If your family doesn't need the money TODAY, Express is wasted money. Save it for real emergencies.

If the name doesn't match character by character with the Guatemalan DPI, the transfer is held and can take days. Copy it exactly — accents, both surnames, everything. Name errors are the #1 cause of held transfers.
Providers charge an extra 2–4% when you pay with credit. On a $200 transfer, that's up to $8 lost. Use debit or ACH (direct bank transfer).

Guatemala has public holidays (September 15, Holy Week) when banks close. If you send the day before with a bank deposit, the money sits in limbo until the next business day. For those dates, cash pickup or a mobile wallet are faster.

faq.title: Frequently asked questions from Guatemalans in the US

items.q: How many quetzales do I get for $200 USD?
items.a: At the current Bank of Guatemala (Banguat) rate, $200 equals the theoretical quetzales shown above multiplied by 200. But each provider applies a margin: the best operator delivers roughly 1–2% less, and the worst up to 3–4% less. Real difference: up to 60 quetzales ($7.85 USD) on a single $200 transfer.

items.q: Which Guatemalan banks receive remittances?
items.a: The main ones are Banrural (nationwide coverage, dominant in rural areas), Banco Industrial (BI), Banco G&T Continental, BAM, and Banco Promerica. All major providers deposit to these. Banrural is especially important for the highland diaspora because of its agency network in small towns.

items.q: Does Tigo Money work everywhere in Guatemala?
items.a: Yes. Tigo Money has coverage in all 22 departments. It's especially useful where there are no nearby bank branches: rural areas of Quiché, Huehuetenango, San Marcos, Petén. Your family withdraws cash at any Tigo Money outlet. Regulated by the Superintendencia de Bancos (SIB).

items.q: Is there a tax on remittances in Guatemala?
items.a: No. Guatemala doesn't tax remittances received. They're exempt from personal income tax (ISR). The only cost is the provider's fee plus the margin on the exchange rate.

items.q: How long does a transfer take to arrive?
items.aIntro: Depends on the method and provider:
aBullets: Cash (Western Union, MoneyGram, Ria): minutes after receiving the MTCN code
Banrural / BI deposit: 2–24 hours depending on the bank

items.q: How much arrives in Guatemala if I send $100 USD?
items.a: At the current Banguat rate, $100 equals the theoretical quetzales shown above multiplied by 100. Providers deliver between 1% and 4% less depending on the operator and method. Difference: up to 30 quetzales ($3.90 USD) per transfer — use the comparison tool to see who delivers more today.

items.q: Which is the best provider to send to Guatemala?
items.a: There's no single "best" — it depends on the receiving bank, amount, urgency, and method. Remitly tends to win for cash pickup on amounts under $500. Wise is typically better for bank deposits thanks to a rate close to the Banguat. Western Union and MoneyGram dominate in rural areas due to agency density. Use the comparison tool above to see who delivers more quetzales today.

cta.titleLine1: Ready to send money to Guatemala.
cta.lede: With what you've learned in this guide, your next transfer to Guatemala can save you $10–$20 vs using the first provider that pops up on Google. Multiplied by 12 transfers a year, that's $120+ staying with your family.
checklist: Confirm which bank your family uses (Banrural dominates rural areas)
Send Monday/Tuesday · avoid Friday/weekends
Debit or ACH > credit card
Look at quetzales received, not just the fee
Confirm the name exactly as on the DPI
Compare every transfer — the best changes week to week

seccion0.alertasSubtitle: Notifications when fees drop or promotions launch

stats.tag: Sending money from the US to El Salvador
stats.title: El Salvador and remittances from the United States in 2026
stats.lede: El Salvador receives $8.5 billion a year from the United States — the second largest corridor in Central America. The country has been dollarized since 2001, so there's no exchange rate to lose in conversion. But fees, delivery methods, and promotions still vary across providers: that's where the difference is. Data from the BCR 2024 and CEPAL 2024.

editorial.tag: The Salvadoran diaspora in the United States
editorial.titleLine1: From Los Angeles to San Salvador.
editorial.titleLine2: From Washington DC to Santa Ana.
editorial.titleLine3: No exchange rate to compare — but there are differences.
editorial.p1: There are 2.5 million Salvadorans in the United States: one of the densest diasporas per capita in all of Latin America. Concentrated in California, Maryland, Texas, Virginia, and New York, they send $8.5 billion home each year. That's 24 cents of every dollar of Salvadoran GDP — one of the highest percentages on the continent.
editorial.p2: El Salvador is the only corridor in the comparison where there's no conversion: USD in, USD out. That simplifies the decision but doesn't eliminate it. Fees vary from $0 to $7 per transfer, fast methods cost $5–$8 extra, and only some providers pay out via ChivoWallet or Tigo Money wallet. Comparing still saves you $5–$10 per transfer.

ciudades.title: Sending money to cities in El Salvador: 6 main areas
ciudades.lede: 6 destinations where your family likely receives. Since the country is small and dollarized, almost all major providers (Remitly, Xoom, WorldRemit, WU, MG) have nationwide coverage. Banco Cuscatlán, Banco Agrícola, and Banco Davivienda dominate deposits. Tigo Money and ChivoWallet operate nationally for cash pickup.

errores.title: Mistakes when sending money to El Salvador that cost up to $50
errores.lede: Each of these mistakes has cost a Salvadoran between $5 and $50. Learn them before your next transfer.

items.titulo: Thinking "USD to USD" is always the same
items.texto: There's no conversion, true — but fees vary from $0 to $7 on a $200 transfer. And "$0 fee" sometimes hides a cost in the delivery method or wait time. Comparing the total cost matters.
"Express" in 5 minutes costs $5–$8 extra. If your family doesn't need the money TODAY, "Economy" in 1 business day is money saved. Save it for real emergencies.

items.titulo: Not checking if your family uses ChivoWallet
items.texto: ChivoWallet is the official government wallet (Bitcoin/USD). Some providers pay out there directly, but not all. If your family is an active user, that saves the trip to the agency. Check which provider lists it as a method.
If the name doesn't match character by character with the Salvadoran DUI, the transfer is held and can take days. Copy it exactly — accents, second surname, everything.
Providers charge an extra 2–4% when you pay with credit because card processors charge them high fees. On a $200 transfer, that's up to $8 lost. Use debit or ACH.

items.titulo: Always sending with the same provider
items.texto: Welcome promotions and "first transfer with $0 fee" rotate among operators. If you only use one, you miss those discounts. Creating accounts at 2–3 lets you take advantage of promos without locking in to any.

faq.title: Frequently asked questions from Salvadorans in the US

items.q: If El Salvador uses USD, why compare providers?
items.a: Good question. The USD/USD conversion is 1.00, but fees vary: from $0 to $7 per transfer. The delivery method (Express vs Economy) costs $5–$8 extra. Welcome and new-customer promotions rotate. Across 12 transfers a year, comparing can save you $60–$120 without touching the "rate." That's what the comparison tool above does.

items.q: Which Salvadoran banks receive remittances?
items.a: The main ones are Banco Cuscatlán, Banco Agrícola, Banco Davivienda, and Banco Promerica. All major providers (Remitly, Xoom, WorldRemit, Wise, WU, MG) deposit to these. Since SV is dollarized, deposits arrive directly as USD with no conversion.

items.q: Do Tigo Money and ChivoWallet work?
items.a: Yes. Tigo Money has nationwide coverage (regulated by the Superintendencia del Sistema Financiero). ChivoWallet is the official government wallet (USD + Bitcoin) launched in 2021 — adoption varies by family. Both work for cash pickup or payments. Check whether your provider includes them as a method.

items.q: Is there a tax on remittances in El Salvador?
items.a: No. El Salvador doesn't tax remittances received. They're exempt from personal income tax (ISR). The only cost is the provider's fee — and since there's no currency conversion, that's all of it.

items.q: Is Bitcoin useful for sending remittances to El Salvador?
items.a: Technically yes since 2021 (it's legal tender), but real adoption for family remittances has been low. Most remittances still arrive as USD via banks, wallets, or cash. If your family isn't an active ChivoWallet user, sending BTC creates extra friction (volatility, conversion, understanding). The comparison tool focuses on USD, which covers ~99% of the corridor.

items.q: How long does a transfer take to arrive?
items.aIntro: Depends on the method and provider:
aBullets: Cash (Western Union, MoneyGram, Ria): minutes after receiving the code

items.q: Which is the best provider to send to El Salvador?
items.a: Since there's no conversion, "best" comes down to fees, speed, and method. Remitly tends to have strong promos for new users and cash pickup. Wise wins on low fees for bank deposits. Western Union remains the option with the most physical agencies. Use the comparison tool above to see who has the best offer today.

cta.titleLine1: Ready to send money to El Salvador.
cta.lede: With what you've learned in this guide, your next transfer to SV can save you $5–$10 vs using the first provider that pops up on Google. Multiplied by 12 transfers a year, that's $60+ staying with your family.
checklist: No conversion: focus on fees and method
Take advantage of new-user promos — they rotate among operators
Economy > Express if there's no rush
Debit or ACH > credit card
Confirm the name exactly as on the DUI
Check if your family uses Tigo Money / ChivoWallet

fuentes.label: Sources:
fuentes.text: Macroeconomic data updated annually. El Salvador has been dollarized since 2001 — no USD/USD conversion. Last content update: {fechaActualizacion}.

seccion0.alertasSubtitle: Notifications when COP/USD goes up — send when it goes farther

stats.tag: Sending money from the US to Colombia
stats.title: Colombia and remittances from the United States in 2026
stats.lede: Colombia receives around $12 billion a year from the United States. It's a corridor with a volatile COP/USD rate — the Colombian peso swings sharply week to week, which means comparing before sending can be worth 100 or 200 pesos per dollar. On a $200 transfer, that's up to 40,000 extra pesos. Data from the Bank of the Republic 2024 and DANE 2024.

editorial.tag: The Colombian diaspora in the United States
editorial.titleLine3: A hundred pesos per dollar sounds like little — multiply it by a thousand.
editorial.p1: There are 1.3 million Colombians in the United States. Most live in Florida (especially Miami), New York, New Jersey, and Texas. Each year they send around $12 billion — the second largest corridor in South America after Mexico. And because the Colombian peso is quoted in the thousands, differences across operators show up in hundreds of pesos per dollar, not in cents.
editorial.p2: In practice that means: the best operator might deliver 4,150 COP per dollar while the worst delivers 4,000. That's 150 pesos of difference per dollar — 30,000 pesos on a $200 transfer. Multiplied by the 8 to 12 transfers many families make each year, that's between $50 and $80 USD staying with your family every year.

ciudades.title: Sending money to cities in Colombia: 6 main areas
ciudades.lede: 6 destinations where your family likely receives. Bancolombia, Davivienda, BBVA Colombia, and Banco de Bogotá dominate deposits. For rural areas, Banco Agrario has the broadest agency network. Operators like Western Union and MoneyGram have agencies in nearly the entire country for cash pickup.

errores.title: Mistakes when sending money to Colombia that cost up to $80
errores.lede: Each of these mistakes has cost a Colombian between $10 and $80. Learn them before your next transfer.

items.titulo: Looking at the "fee" without looking at the rate
items.texto: In Colombia, pesos per dollar vary 100–200 across operators. "$0 fee" can hide a worse rate that costs 30,000 pesos on a $200 transfer. Always look at the final pesos received, not the fee alone.

items.titulo: Sticking with the first provider you tried
items.texto: The best provider for Colombia changes week to week depending on the COP/USD rate from the Bank of the Republic and on promotions. Comparing every time saves you $5–$10 per transfer in Colombian pesos.

items.titulo: Paying Express when there's no rush
items.texto: "Express" in 5 minutes costs $5–$8 extra vs "Economy" in 1 day. If your family doesn't need the money TODAY, Express is wasted money. Save it for real emergencies.

items.titulo: Forgetting the receiving bank's automatic charge
items.texto: Some Colombian banks charge a small fee when they receive an international transfer ($1–$3 USD deducted on receipt). That depends on the bank, not on the provider. Check with your family whether Bancolombia / BBVA / Davivienda charges them anything on receipt.
Providers charge an extra 2–4% when you pay with credit because card processors charge them high fees. On a $200 transfer, that's up to $8 lost. Use debit or ACH.

items.titulo: Not verifying the Colombian ID number
items.texto: Colombia uses a citizen ID (NUIP). If the number or name doesn't match exactly with the ID, the transfer is held. Ask your family for the exact number before sending — one wrong digit means days of delay.

faq.title: Frequently asked questions from Colombians in the US

items.q: How many Colombian pesos do I get for $200 USD?
items.a: At the current Bank of the Republic rate, $200 equals the theoretical pesos shown above multiplied by 200. But each provider applies a margin: the best operator delivers roughly 1–2% less, and the worst up to 3–4% less. Real difference: up to 30,000 pesos ($7.20 USD) on a single $200 transfer.

items.q: Which Colombian banks receive remittances?
items.a: The main ones are Bancolombia, Davivienda, BBVA Colombia, Banco de Bogotá, Banco Caja Social, and Banco Agrario (the latter with broader coverage in rural areas). All major providers (Remitly, Xoom, WorldRemit, Wise) deposit to these accounts. If your family doesn't have an account, cash pickup at WU/MG is the alternative.

items.q: Does my Colombian bank charge me on receipt?
items.a: Some do, others don't. Bancolombia, BBVA Colombia, and Davivienda typically charge a fee of around 1,000–10,000 pesos on receiving an international transfer. Other banks don't charge. This depends on the receiving bank, NOT on the provider. Check with your family before sending.

items.q: Is there a tax on remittances in Colombia?
items.a: No. Colombia doesn't tax remittances received. They're exempt from withholding tax and from VAT. The only cost is the provider's fee plus the margin on the exchange rate (and the occasional charge from the receiving bank for receiving SWIFT).

items.q: How long does a transfer take to arrive?
items.aIntro: Depends on the method and provider:
aBullets: Cash (Western Union, Ria, MoneyGram): minutes after receiving the code
Nequi (digital wallet): seconds at many providers

items.q: How much arrives in Colombia if I send $100 USD?
items.a: At the current Bank of the Republic rate, $100 equals the theoretical pesos shown above multiplied by 100. Providers deliver between 1% and 4% less depending on the operator and method. Difference: up to 15,000 pesos ($3.60 USD) per transfer — use the comparison tool to see who delivers more today.

items.q: Which is the best provider to send to Colombia?
items.a: There's no single "best" — it depends on the amount, urgency, and method. Remitly tends to win for amounts under $500 with cash pickup or Nequi. Wise is typically better for bank deposits thanks to a rate close to the Bank of the Republic. Xoom and WorldRemit are competitive with promotions for new users. Western Union dominates in remote areas with its agency network. Use the comparison tool above to see who delivers more pesos today.

cta.titleLine1: Ready to send money to Colombia.
cta.lede: With what you've learned in this guide, your next transfer to Colombia can save you $8–$15 vs using the first provider that pops up on Google. Multiplied by 12 transfers a year, that's $100+ staying with your family.
checklist: Look at Colombian pesos received, not the "fee"
Check whether your bank charges on receipt
Send Monday/Tuesday · avoid Friday/weekends
Debit or ACH > credit card
Confirm citizen ID with no wrong digit
Compare every transfer — the best changes week to week

fuentes.text: Macroeconomic data updated annually. COP/USD rate updated daily. Last content update: {fechaActualizacion}.

seccion0.alertasSubtitle: Notifications when MXN/USD goes up — send when it goes farther

stats.tag: Sending money from the US to Mexico
stats.title: Mexico and remittances from the United States in 2026
stats.lede: Mexico is the largest remittance corridor in the world: $66 billion a year from the United States. Behind every dollar are 37 million Mexicans and Mexican-Americans supporting families across all 32 states. When volumes are this large, even a 0.5% rate difference adds up to hundreds of dollars a year. Data from Banxico 2024 and the World Bank 2024.

editorial.tag: The Mexican diaspora in the United States
editorial.titleLine3: The world's largest corridor — the math of the 0.5%.
editorial.p1: There are 37 million Mexicans and Mexican-Americans in the United States: 1 in every 9 residents of the country. Each year they send $66 billion home — more than any other remittance corridor on the planet. Those pesos sustain homes in Jalisco, milpas in Oaxaca, schools in Puebla, businesses in Monterrey, and medical care all over the country.
editorial.p2: With volume like that, the details matter. The best operator might deliver 17.10 pesos per dollar while the worst delivers 16.85. That's 25 cents of difference per dollar — $50 pesos on a $200 transfer. Applied to the typical 12 transfers a year, that's $600 pesos a year ($35 USD) staying with your family instead of paying margin to the operator.

ciudades.title: Sending money to cities in Mexico: 6 main areas
ciudades.lede: 6 representative destinations. But Mexico is huge — the major providers (Remitly, Xoom, WorldRemit, WU, MG) cover all 32 states with accounts at BBVA México, Santander, Banamex, HSBC, and Banorte. For rural areas, Western Union, MoneyGram, and Elektra have the densest agency network for cash pickup.

errores.title: Mistakes when sending money to Mexico that cost up to $100
errores.lede: Each of these mistakes has cost a Mexican between $10 and $100. Learn them before your next transfer.

items.titulo: Sticking with the first provider out of habit
items.texto: The best provider changes week to week depending on the Mexican peso (which moves a lot) and on promotions. If you've been using the same one for years, you're probably leaving $5–$10 per transfer on the table. Comparing every time is rule #1.

items.titulo: Confusing "$0 fee" with a free transfer
items.texto: "$0 fee" doesn't mean a free transfer. The provider makes up for it with a worse exchange rate — you end up receiving fewer Mexican pesos. Look at the final pesos received, not the fee alone.

items.titulo: Paying Express when there's no rush
items.texto: "Express" in 5 minutes costs $5–$8 extra vs "Economy" in 1 day. If your family doesn't need the money TODAY, Express is wasted money. Save it for real emergencies.

If the name doesn't match character by character with the Mexican INE/IFE, the transfer is held and can take days. Copy it exactly — accents, both surnames, everything. Name errors are the #1 cause of held transfers.
Providers charge an extra 2–4% when you pay with credit because card processors charge them high fees. On a $200 transfer, that's up to $8 lost. Use debit or ACH.

For a bank deposit in Mexico you need the interbank CLABE (18 digits), not just the account number. If your family gives you only the 11-digit account, the transfer bounces or arrives more slowly. Always ask for the full CLABE.

faq.title: Frequently asked questions from Mexicans in the US

items.q: How many Mexican pesos do I get for $200 USD?
items.a: At the current Bank of Mexico (Banxico) rate, $200 equals the theoretical pesos shown above multiplied by 200. But each provider applies a margin: the best operator delivers roughly 1–2% less, and the worst up to 3–4% less. Real difference: up to 50 pesos ($2.95 USD) on a single $200 transfer.

items.q: Which Mexican banks receive remittances?
items.a: The main ones are BBVA México (the largest), Santander, Citibanamex, Banorte, and HSBC. All major providers deposit to these via CLABE. For cash pickup, Elektra is Western Union's classic partner (with thousands of stores across the country); it also works at Telecomm-Telégrafos branches in rural areas.

items.q: What is the CLABE and why do I need it?
items.a: The CLABE (Standardized Banking Code) is a unique 18-digit number for every Mexican bank account. It's what providers need to deposit — not the short account number. Your family can find it on their bank statement or app. Without the correct CLABE, the transfer bounces.

items.q: Is there a tax on remittances in Mexico?
items.a: No at the federal level. Mexico doesn't tax remittances received under personal income tax (ISR). But heads up: in 2025, the US Congress discussed a possible 1–5% tax on the sender side (not the recipient). Check whether your provider is already applying it — some break it out clearly, others don't. The comparison tool above shows the net amount.

items.q: How long does a transfer take to arrive?
items.aIntro: Depends on the method and provider:
aBullets: Cash (Western Union via Elektra, MoneyGram, Ria): minutes after receiving the code
SPEI (Mexican interbank transfer): seconds to minutes

items.q: How much arrives in Mexico if I send $100 USD?
items.a: At the current Banxico rate, $100 equals the theoretical pesos shown above multiplied by 100. Providers deliver between 1% and 4% less depending on the operator and method. Difference: up to 25 pesos ($1.50 USD) per transfer — small in absolute terms, but accumulated across 12 transfers a year that's $20+. Use the comparison tool to see who delivers more today.

items.q: Which is the best provider to send to Mexico?
items.a: There's no single "best" — it depends on the amount, urgency, and method. Remitly tends to win for amounts under $500 with cash pickup at Elektra. Wise is typically better for bank deposits via CLABE thanks to a rate close to Banxico. Xoom (from PayPal) has a strong presence and recurring promotions. Western Union dominates cash pickup with its agency density. Use the comparison tool above to see who delivers more pesos today.

cta.titleLine1: Ready to send money to Mexico.
cta.lede: With what you've learned in this guide, your next transfer to Mexico can save you $5–$15 vs using the first provider that pops up on Google. Multiplied by the typical 12+ transfers a year, that's $100+ staying with your family.
checklist: Ask for the 18-digit CLABE, not the short account
Look at Mexican pesos received, not just the fee
Send Monday/Tuesday · avoid Friday/weekends
Debit or ACH > credit card
Confirm the name exactly as on the INE/IFE
Compare every transfer — the best changes week to week
